head: die BΟΦrokratie /by ¹ok ¹aΥàtiΥê/
notes: βÄΔ Correct spelling: BΟΦrokratie, not Burokrative. It has ΟΦ and ends in -ie.

examples.german: Die BΟΦrokratie in Deutschland ist manchmal kompliziert.
examples.english: The bureaucracy in Germany is sometimes complicated.
We are learning words about bureaucracy.

head: von A bis Z /f…în aΥê b…Σs ts…¦t/
gloss: From A to Z; completely, start to finish.
notes: βÄΔ Fixed phrase: von ... bis ... = from ... to/until ...
βÄΔ German says Z as zett. Von A bis Z means the whole topic, from beginning to end.

examples.german: Wir erklΟΛren das Thema von A bis Z.
examples.english: We explain the topic from start to finish.

head: tauschen /Υàta äΧ· É…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Formal command: Tauschen Sie ... = Exchange ... / Swap ...; verb first, Sie capitalized.
βÄΔ With tauschen, the thing exchanged is accusative. For plural nouns, nominative and accusative look the same: die ... stays die ....

examples.german: Tauschen Sie die Notizzettel im Kurs.
examples.english: Exchange the note sheets in class.

examples.german: Tauschen Sie die Notizzettel. Fragen Sie: Wie ist die Telefonnummer von Anne?
examples.english: Exchange the note sheets. Ask: What is Anne's phone number?

examples.german: Tauschen Sie die Antworten mit einem Partner.
examples.english: Exchange the answers with a partner. [formal you]

head: der Kurs /k ä ¹s/
notes: βÄΔ im Kurs = in dem Kurs = in class / in the course.
βÄΔ Location with in uses dative: in dem Kurs becomes im Kurs.

examples.german: Tauschen Sie die Notizzettel im Kurs.
examples.english: Exchange the note sheets in class.
I am going to the course.

head: die Handynummer /Υàh…¦ndiΥ¨n äm…ê/
gloss: Mobile phone number; cell phone number.
notes: βÄΔ das Handy = mobile phone / cell phone; die Nummer = number as an ID/code.
βÄΔ die Handynummer is specifically a mobile number; die Telefonnummer can be any phone number.

examples.english: Do you have Anne's mobile number?

head: die Zahlen ab 11 /diΥê ΥàtsaΥêl…ôn ap …¦lf/
notes: βÄΔ 13-19 mostly use -zehn: dreizehn, vierzehn, fΟΦnfzehn; watch the shortened forms sechzehn and siebzehn.
βÄΔ 21-99 put the small number first, then und, then the ten: einundzwanzig = one-and-twenty = twenty-one.
βÄΔ In listening, zwo may be used for zwei, hundert for einhundert, and tausend for eintausend.
βÄΔ Million and Milliarde are nouns, so they use eine: eine Million, eine Milliarde.

head: mehr /meΥê…êΧ·/
notes: βÄΔ mehr adds quantity: mehr Zahlen = more numbers.
βÄΔ Opposite idea: weniger = less / fewer.

examples.english: More numbers again? / Even more numbers?

head: noch einmal /n…îx Υàa…ΣΧ·nmaΥêl/
notes: βÄΔ noch = still / yet / additionally; einmal = once / one time.
βÄΔ Together, noch einmal means one more time or again. Casual shorter form: nochmal.

examples.german: HΟΕren Sie das GesprΟΛch noch einmal.
examples.english: Listen to the conversation one more time.

examples.german: Bitte wiederholen Sie das noch einmal.
examples.english: Please repeat that one more time.
More numbers again? / Even more numbers?

head: der Notizzettel /noΥàtiΥêtsΥ¨ts…¦tlΧ©/
notes: βÄΔ Correct spelling: Notizzettel, not notizzetel/nottizetel.
βÄΔ die Notiz = note; der Zettel = slip/piece of paper. Together: der Notizzettel = a note sheet.

examples.german: ErgΟΛnzen Sie den Notizzettel. (den because masculine + accusative.)
HΟΕren Sie das GesprΟΛch noch einmal und ergΟΛnzen Sie den Notizzettel.
examples.english: Listen to the conversation one more time and complete the note sheet.

examples.german: Der Notizzettel liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.english: The note slip is on the table.

head: TschΟΦss /t É ès/
notes: βÄΔ TschΟΦss is the common German casual goodbye, especially in everyday speech.

head: Tschau /t Éa ä/
notes: βÄΔ Tschau is the German spelling of Italian ciao; it is casual, like English bye.
βÄΔ TschΟΦss and Tschau feel similar because both are casual goodbyes and both start with tsch, but they are different words.

head: spΟΛter /Υà Ép…¦Υêt…ê/
notes: βÄΔ Opposite idea: frΟΦh = early; spΟΛter = later. Related adjective: spΟΛt = late.
βÄΔ In bis spΟΛter, German literally says until later; natural English is see you later.

examples.english: Early today, later in the evening.

head: bis /b…Σs/
notes: βÄΔ In farewell formulas, bis means until: bis spΟΛter = until later / see you later.

head: dann /dan/
notes: βÄΔ Use dann to order actions in time: first one thing, then the next.
βÄΔ In a normal statement, dann can stand first; the conjugated verb then comes right after it.

examples.german: Zuerst lernen wir Zahlen, dann lesen wir den Text.
examples.english: First we learn numbers, then we read the text.

examples.german: Ich trinke Kaffee, dann gehe ich zum Kurs.
examples.english: I drink coffee, then I go to the course.

examples.german: Wenn du Zeit hast, dann komm bitte.
examples.english: If you have time, then please come.

head: die Vorwahl /ΥàfoΥê…êΧ·vaΥêl/
notes: βÄΔ One compound word: Vorwahl, not two words.
βÄΔ In 0621 / 53487, 0621 is the Vorwahl and 53487 is the local number.

examples.english: What area code does Berlin have?

head: kΟΕnnen /Υàk≈™n…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Modal-verb pattern: the conjugated modal verb takes the normal verb position, and the main verb stays as an infinitive at the end.
βÄΔ In yes/no questions, the conjugated modal comes first: Kannst du ... wiederholen?
βÄΔ In Kannst du das bitte wiederholen?, das is the object (ΌÖΌ¹ΊΙΌàΌ³) of the sentence (Ί§ΌÖΌ³Ό΅): the thing being repeated. Bitte means please and can move around.

examples.english: Can you please speak slowly? [formal you]

head: wiederholen /viΥêd…êΥàhoΥêl…ôn/
gloss: To repeat; say or do again.

head: wieder /ΥàviΥêd…ê/
notes: βÄΔ Do not confuse wieder (again) with wider (against).

examples.german: Bis morgen! Wir sehen uns wieder.
examples.english: See you tomorrow! We'll see each other again.

head: die Telefonnummer /teΥàlefoΥênΥ¨n äm…ê/
notes: βÄΔ die Nummer + das Telefon becomes die Telefonnummer: a compound noun with the gender of Nummer.
βÄΔ In German, ask Wie ist deine Telefonnummer? literally: How is your phone number?

examples.german: Das ist die Telefonnummer von Maria.

head: die StraΟüe /Υà Ét ¹aΥês…ô/
notes: βÄΔ Standard German spelling uses Οü: StraΟüe. In Switzerland, it is usually written Strasse.
βÄΔ In der BergstraΟüe uses Dativ: wohnen answers where?; with location, in + feminine die StraΟüe becomes in der StraΟüe.

examples.german: Ich wohne in Mannheim in der BergstraΟüe 10.
examples.english: I live in Mannheim at 10 BergstraΟüe.

head: wohnen /ΥàvoΥên…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Use wohnen for where someone lives: Ich wohne in Berlin.
βÄΔ Common question: Wo wohnen Sie? = Where do you live? [formal you]

head: gern /…Γ…¦ ¹n/
notes: βÄΔ gern and gerne mean the same thing; gerne sounds a little fuller/softer.
βÄΔ With a verb, gern often translates naturally as like to: Ich lerne gern = I like learning.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.

head: zum /ts äm/
gloss: To the; for the; contraction of zu + dem.
notes: βÄΔ zum = zu + dem, used before masculine or neuter dative nouns.
βÄΔ In zum Essen, Essen is used as a noun: for the meal / to eat.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.
I am going to the course.

head: essen /Υà…¦snΧ©/
gloss: To eat; food or meal when capitalized as Essen.
notes: βÄΔ Lowercase essen is the verb: to eat.
βÄΔ Capitalized Essen is a noun: food or a meal. So write zum Essen, not zum essen.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.

head: die Zahlen 0-10 /diΥê ΥàtsaΥêl…ôn n äl b…Σs tseΥên/
notes: βÄΔ eins is the counting form for 1; before a noun it usually becomes ein/eine.
βÄΔ In phone numbers, digits are often spoken one by one: 0612 = null sechs eins zwei.

examples.german: Meine Telefonnummer ist null sechs eins zwei.
examples.english: My phone number is zero six one two.

head: die Nummer /Υàn äm…ê/
gloss: Number as a label, code, or identifier.
notes: βÄΔ Nummer is a number used as an ID or label: Telefonnummer, Hausnummer, Zimmer Nummer 12.
βÄΔ Difference: Zahl = numerical value/counting/math; Nummer = assigned number/code/identifier.

head: die Zahl /tsaΥêl/
notes: βÄΔ Zahl is a number as a value: counting, math, or a number you hear/write.
βÄΔ Compare Nummer: an assigned label/code/ID, as in Telefonnummer, Hausnummer, Zimmer Nummer 12.

head: von /f…în/
notes: βÄΔ In die Adresse von Anne, von shows whose address it is: Anne's address.
βÄΔ von always takes dative, but with names like Anne there is no visible article change.

examples.german: Die Adresse von Anne ist hier.
Das ist die Telefonnummer von Maria.

head: stehen /Υà ÉteΥê…ôn/
gloss: To stand; be located or positioned.
notes: βÄΔ In word-order rules, stehen means a word βÄ€standsβÄù or βÄ€goesβÄù in a certain sentence position.
βÄΔ Das Verb steht auf Position 1 means the verb comes first; Position 2 means it comes second.

examples.german: In Ja-/Nein-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 1.
examples.english: In yes/no questions, the verb comes first.

examples.german: In Antworten und W-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 2.
examples.english: In answers and W-questions, the verb comes second.
A new car is parked there.

head: die Regel /Υà ¹eΥê…ΓlΧ©/
notes: βÄΔ die Regeln is plural accusative here: the rules being completed.

head: schreiben /Υà É ¹a…ΣΧ·b…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Formal command: Schreiben Sie ... = Write ...; verb first, Sie capitalized.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Write that in the table. [formal you]

head: der Satz /zats/
notes: βÄΔ die SΟΛtze is plural accusative here: the sentences being written.
βÄΔ Correct spelling: SΟΛtze with ΟΛ, not Satze.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die SΟΛtze noch einmal.
examples.english: Read the sentences one more time. [formal you]

head: LΟΛnder, Sprachen, NationalitΟΛten /Υàl…¦nd…ê Υà Ép ¹aΥêx…ôn natsiΧ·onaliΥàt…¦Υêt…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Nationality nouns are capitalized in German: der Schweizer, die Schweizerin.
βÄΔ Textbook shorthand Spanier/in means der Spanier / die Spanierin.

examples.german: Sie kommt aus Frankreich und spricht FranzΟΕsisch.
examples.english: She comes from France and speaks French.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin.
examples.english: He is Swiss; she is Swiss.

examples.german: Er ist Spanier, sie ist Spanierin.
examples.english: He is Spanish; she is Spanish.

head: Viel SpaΟü! /fiΥêl  ÉpaΥês/
notes: βÄΔ Correct spelling in standard German: SpaΟü with Οü, not spas.

head: der SpaΟü / ÉpaΥês/
notes: βÄΔ Long a + sharp s: SpaΟü. In Switzerland, Οü is usually written ss: Spass.

examples.english: That is a lot of fun.

head: viel /fiΥêl/
gloss: Much; a lot; many before plural nouns.
notes: βÄΔ In Viel SpaΟü!, viel intensifies the wish: literally much fun, naturally have fun.

examples.english: I have a lot of time.

head: zusammen /tsuΥàzam…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ Spelling: zwei m und -en at the end βÄî not zusamen (common typo/OCR).
βÄΔ In Hoi zusammen!, zusammen means βÄ€everyone / all of youβÄù βÄî like English βÄ€Hi all!βÄù

head: Hoi /h…î…Σ/
gloss: Informal hi or hey; especially typical in Switzerland.
notes: βÄΔ One-word greeting (interjection), like Hallo or Servus βÄî no article, no plural, no verb endings.
βÄΔ Very common in Swiss German contexts; also heard in casual speech elsewhere; not the standard classroom default.

head: die Argentinierin /a ¹…Γ…¦nΥàtsiΥên…ô ¹…Σn/
gloss: An Argentine; feminine die Argentinierin, masculine der Argentinier.
notes: βÄΔ Same pattern as die Schweizerin (from die Schweiz): demonyms from Argentinien, the country name in German.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin; er ist Argentinier, sie ist Argentinierin.
examples.english: He is Swiss, she is Swiss; he is Argentine, she is Argentine.

examples.german: Sie ist Argentinierin und lebt in Berlin.
examples.english: She is Argentine and lives in Berlin.

examples.german: Zwei Argentinierinnen und ein Argentinier arbeiten hier.
examples.english: Two Argentine women and one Argentine man work here.

head: Servus /Υàz…¦…êΧ·vus/
gloss: Informal hi or bye; strongest in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: βÄΔ Informal greeting and goodbye; most typical in Austria, Bavaria, and other southern regions.
βÄΔ From Latin for βÄ€your servantβÄù; today it is a casual hi, hey, or bye in one short word.
βÄΔ Not the everyday norm everywhere in Germany; in the north people more often say Hallo or TschΟΦss.

head: in /…Σn/
gloss: In; inside; on a team (+ dative for location).
notes: βÄΔ Wo? uses dative: in einem Team. Wohin? uses accusative: in ein Team.

examples.english: You all are on a team.
You all are joining / going into a team. [accusative: direction]

head: der neue Praktikant /deΥê…êΧ· Υàn…î è…ô p ¹aktiΥàkant/
gloss: The new intern; pattern for adjective endings after der.
notes: βÄΔ neu becomes neue because it stands before a masculine nominative noun with der.
βÄΔ After der/die/das, use -e in nominative singular; use -en in most other definite-article forms.

examples.english: The intern is new. [after sein: no adjective ending]
I see the new intern. [accusative masculine: den neuen]

head: auch /a äΧ·x/
notes: βÄΔ Often comes near what it adds: Ich auch = me too; auch ich = I too / even I.

head: Wie gehtβÄôs? /viΥê …ΓeΥêts/
gloss: HowβÄôs it going? / How are you?
notes: βÄΔ Short for: Wie geht es? Full informal form: Wie geht es dir?
βÄΔ Common response: Und dir? (= Und wie geht es dir?)
βÄΔ Another common response: Mir gehtβÄôs gut. (= IβÄôm doing well.)
βÄΔ Formally: Wie geht es Ihnen?

examples.english: HowβÄôs it going? / How are you?

head: Freut mich /f ¹…î èΧ·t m…ΣΟß/
gloss: Nice to meet you; pleased to meet you.
notes: βÄΔ Short for Es freut mich: literally, it pleases me.
βÄΔ mich is a personal pronoun in the accusative here, not reflexive; the hidden subject is es.
βÄΔ Contrast: Ich freue mich means I am happy / pleased; there mich is reflexive because ich and mich refer to the same person.

examples.english: Nice to meet you. [personal-pronoun mich, accusative]

examples.german: Guten Tag, ich heiΟüe Ali. Freut mich.
examples.english: Good day, my name is Ali. Nice to meet you.

head: Reflexivpronomen / ¹efl…¦ΥàksiΥêfΥ¨p ¹oΥênoΥêm…ôn/
gloss: Reflexive pronouns: myself, yourself, himself, etc.
notes: βÄΔ Reflexive means the subject and object are the same person: Ich wasche mich.
βÄΔ They follow person and case, not grammatical gender: er/sie/es all use sich.
βÄΔ Akkusativ forms: mich, dich, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
βÄΔ Dativ forms: mir, dir, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
βÄΔ Use accusative when the reflexive pronoun is the direct object; use dative when another accusative object is present: Ich wasche mich vs. Ich wasche mir die HΟΛnde.

examples.english: You all introduce yourselves. [euch; accusative reflexive]
I wash my hands. [mir; dative reflexive]
You buy yourself a book. [dir; dative reflexive]
She buys herself a coffee. [sich; dative reflexive]
It washes its hands. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Wir stellen uns eine Reise vor.
examples.english: We imagine a trip. [uns; dative reflexive]
You all buy yourselves tickets. [euch; dative reflexive]
They buy themselves tickets. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.
examples.english: Imagine a city. [sich; formal dative reflexive]

head: Personalpronomen /p…¦ ¹zoΥànaΥêlΥ¨p ¹oΥênoΥêm…ôn/
gloss: Personal pronouns: ich, mich, mir; du, dich, dir; etc.
notes: βÄΔ Pronouns replace nouns: der Mann βÜ£ er; die Frau βÜ£ sie; das Kind βÜ£ es.
βÄΔ Nominativ marks the subject: ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie.
βÄΔ Akkusativ marks the direct object: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie.
βÄΔ Dativ marks the indirect object or object after dative verbs/prepositions: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen.
βÄΔ Reflexive pronouns can look the same as personal pronouns, but the subject and object are the same person: Ich liebe dich vs. Du liebst dich.

examples.english: I help it. [ihm; dative for a neuter noun]
They come from Germany. [sie; nominative plural]

head: sich vorstellen /z…ΣΟß ΥàfoΥê…êΧ·Υ¨ Ét…¦l…ôn/
gloss: To introduce oneself; to imagine something.
notes: βÄΔ Separable reflexive verb: vor moves to the end (Ich stelle mich vor.).
βÄΔ Introduce oneself uses accusative reflexive pronouns: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich.
βÄΔ Imagine something uses dative reflexive pronouns with an object: mir, dir, sich, uns, euch, sich.

examples.german: Wir stellen uns im Kurs vor.
Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.

head: begrΟΦΟüen /b…ôΥà…Γ ¹yΥêsnΧ©/
gloss: To greet; say hello to someone.
notes: βÄΔ Takes an accusative object: BegrΟΦΟüen Sie Ihren Partner / Ihre Partnerin.

examples.english: Greet your partner. [male partner; formal instruction]
Greet your partner. [female partner; formal instruction]

head: der Partner / die Partnerin /Υàpa ¹tn…ê/ /Υàpa ¹tn…ô ¹…Σn/

examples.english: Your partner is here. [male partner; nominative]

head: Wie heiΟüt ...? /viΥê ha…ΣΧ·st/
notes: βÄΔ W-question pattern: Wie is position 1, the finite verb heiΟüt is position 2.

examples.german: Wie heiΟüt Ihr Land in Ihrer Sprache?
examples.english: What is your country called in your language? [formal]
What is that called in German?

head: die Sprache /Υà Ép ¹aΥêx…ô/
notes: βÄΔ Feminine noun: die Sprache; after static in, use dative: in der Sprache / in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.german: In Ihrer Sprache heiΟüt das anders.
examples.english: In your language, that is called something different. [formal]
I am learning the German language.

head: Ihr- /iΥê…êΧ·/
notes: βÄΔ Capitalized Ihr- means formal your. Endings follow ein-words: Ihr Land, Ihre Sprache, in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.english: I am learning your language. [formal; accusative]
I speak in your language. [formal; dative]

head: fliegen /ΥàfliΥê…Γ…ôn/
gloss: To fly; travel by plane (+ nach βÄΠ / zu βÄΠ).

head: reisen /Υà ¹a…ΣΧ·znΧ©/
gloss: To travel; go on a trip (+ nach βÄΠ without article; + in die βÄΠ when an article appears).

head: fahren /ΥàfaΥê ¹…ôn/
gloss: To drive; ride; go by transport (+ nach βÄΠ for many destinations).
notes: βÄΔ Strong verb: er fΟΛhrt; PrΟΛteritum fuhr βÄî expect this in texts and exams.

examples.english: We're going to Munich by car / bus / train.
Where are you going? [formal, destination ask]

head: gehen /Υà…ΓeΥê…ôn/
gloss: To go; walk (+ zu / nach / + place; also set phrases).
notes: βÄΔ Irregular present: ich gehe, du gehst, er geht; for whole countries people often prefer fahren/reisen/fliegen.

head: aber /ΥàaΥêb…ê/
gloss: but; however βÄî introduces a contrast or exception
notes: βÄΔ Coordinating conjunction: word order in the following clause is unchanged (verb stays in position 2).

examples.german: Ich komme aus Deutschland, aber er kommt aus der Schweiz.
examples.english: I come from Germany, but he comes from Switzerland.

examples.german: Die meisten LΟΛnder haben keinen Artikel βÄî aber die Schweiz, die TΟΦrkei und die Niederlande schon.
examples.english: Most countries have no article βÄî but Switzerland, Turkey, and the Netherlands do.

head: auffallen /Υàa äΧ·fΥ¨fal…ôn/
gloss: To stand out; be noticeable / catch the eye.
notes: βÄΔ Separable: auf sits at the end of the clause (Was fΟΛllt auf?).

examples.english: What do you notice? / What stands out? [reflection question]
The photo stands out; it catches your eye.

head: markieren /ma ¹ΥàkiΥê ¹…ôn/
gloss: To mark; highlight verbs or phrases in the text (+ acc.).

examples.german: Markieren Sie den Infinitiv im Text.
examples.english: Mark the infinitive in the passage [instruction].

head: heiΟüen /Υàha…ΣΧ·snΧ©/
gloss: To be called; oneβÄôs name is (3rd sg. er/sie/es heiΟüt).
notes: βÄΔ OCR often miswrites heiΟüen as heisen; spelling is heiΟüen (Οü after long diphthong).

head: die W-Frage /ΥàveΥêΥ¨fraΥê…Γ…ô/
gloss: WH-question: starts with Wer, Was, Wo, wie, Wann βÄΠ
notes: βÄΔ The question word fills slot 1; the finite verb is still second (verb-second), then the rest.

head: die Antwort /ΥàantΥ¨v…î ¹t/
gloss: Answer; reply (to a question or task).

examples.german: Schreiben Sie die Antwort in den Satz.
examples.english: Write the answer into the sentence.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table [class instruction].

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.

head: die Verbform /Υàf…î ¹m/
gloss: Verb form (inflected shape of a lemma).
notes: βÄΔ Misread Verbendung: the word is Verbformen.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

head: auf einen Blick /a äΧ·f Υàa…ΣΧ·n…ôn Υàbl…Σk/

examples.german: Das ist die Ο€bersicht auf einen Blick.
examples.english: That's the overview at a glance.

head: das Verb /v…¦ ¹p/
gloss: Verb (word class for actions and states).

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die SekretΟΛrin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: die Frau /fra äΧ·/
gloss: Ms. or Mrs. before a surname.

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die SekretΟΛrin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: aus /a äΧ·s/
gloss: From; out of (+ dative for origin).

head: kommen /Υàk…îm…ôn/
gloss: To come; aus βÄΠ = from (a place).

head: GrΟΦΟü Gott! /Υà…Γ ¹ ès Υà…Γ…ît/
gloss: Polite daytime helloβÄîtypical in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: βÄΔ Elsewhere Guten Tag is often the default; Hallo is informal.

examples.german: Lesen Sie das GesprΟΛch und ergΟΛnzen Sie.
examples.english: Read the dialogue [aloud/on the page], then finish / fill it in.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die Ο€berschrift zu Beginn der Ο€bung.
examples.english: Read the headline at the start of the exercise.
Two children listen to the dialogue.

examples.german: Das Bild passt zu diesem Text.
examples.english: The picture goes with / fits this text passage.
The second photo does not fit / is wrong.
The photo is sharp / in focus.

head: ankreuzen /Υàank ¹…î èts…ôn/
notes: βÄΔ The prefix an- moves to the end of the clause: Kreuzen Sie die richtige LΟΕsung an.
βÄΔ Participle angekreuzt (perfect often: habe βÄΠ angekreuzt).
